hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Semi-detached, Bay-and-Gable (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a semi-detached pair, they retain most of their original building features including their shared roofline, and mirrored facades featuring brick cladding with decorative brickwork, flat headed window and door openings, stone lintels, and double-height projecting bay windows set under gables with decorative woodwork. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

context.street_character: One-way | Residential | Active laneway housing development

facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double-hung sash windows, and 1 principal entrance.
facade_detail.heritage_expression: Character-defining facade elements include original windows, bay window, decorative brick.

Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street.

facade_description: 3 Wales Ave presents a bay-and-gable domestic facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its semi-detached bay-and-gable house-form typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double-hung sash windows, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include original windows, bay window, decorative brick. HCD note: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a semi-detached pair, they retain most of their original building features including their shared roofline, and mirrored facades featuring brick cladding with decorative brickwork, flat headed window and door openings, stone lintels, and double-height projecting bay windows set under gables with decorative woodwork. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.
